output:
# SIEVE — Sequential-Information Estimation & Verification for Evaluation

## 1. Literature Review

Sequential recommendation (SRS) presumes its benchmarks actually contain the order-dependent signal its models exploit. Klenitskiy, Volodkevich, Pembek & Vasilev (RecSys '24 LBR, arXiv:2408.12008) tested that premise with a shuffle diagnostic — comparing a metric on shuffled vs. unshuffled user sequences via three estimators (model-agnostic sequential rules; SASRec and GRU4Rec trained on shuffled vs. unshuffled data) — and found Foursquare, Gowalla, RetailRocket, Steam, and Yelp carry little sequential structure. The dominant SRS architectures (GRU4Rec, Hidasi et al., ICLR 2016; SASRec, Kang & McAuley, ICDM 2018; BERT4Rec, Sun et al., CIKM 2019; SR-GNN, Wu et al., AAAI 2019) advance modeling capacity but assume the data is sequential and report only aggregate accuracy. A parallel reproducibility literature shows benchmarks and protocols, not models, often drive headline numbers (Ludewig & Jannach, UMUAI 2018; Ferrari Dacrema, Cremonesi & Jannach, RecSys 2019; Krichene & Rendle, KDD 2020; Rendle, Krichene, Zhang & Anderson, RecSys 2020), and that fair training shrinks supposed gaps between sequential models (Klenitskiy & Vasilev, RecSys 2023). The information-theoretic and Markov-order tools for quantifying how much order adds predictively exist in time-series, NLP, and genomics, but have not been ported to the sparse, high-cardinality, short-sequence regime of recommendation.

## 2. Brainstorming

The team weighed four directions. **(A) SIEVE** — an instance-level, decomposable order-dependence measure that generalizes the shuffle test into a predictive-information gap, adds nested-null signal decomposition, a per-sequence routing score, and an oracle headroom bound. **(B)** A curated "sequentiality-audited" benchmark suite — rejected as descriptive, a dataset artifact rather than a reusable method. **(C)** A causal/interventional order test via counterfactual reordering — not discarded but *folded into* SIEVE's nested-null design, where each null is precisely such an intervention. **(D)** Order-aware data augmentation to *inject* sequential signal — rejected because it changes the data instead of measuring the property in question. We chose SIEVE because it is the most novel, feasible, and actionable option and strictly generalizes the source paper (whose shuffle test becomes a boundary case), rather than merely cataloguing or perturbing datasets.

## 3. Summary of Collective Ideas

SIEVE reframes "is this dataset sequential?" as "how, where, and how much?" by measuring a matched-model predictive-information gap and turning it into a decision instrument. Its four core components are: (1) the gap **Δ = I(next ; order-aware history) − I(next ; order-agnostic multiset)**, estimated from held-out cross-entropy in nats; (2) a **nested-null decomposition** (shuffle-all, window-shuffle, de-repeat) attributing Δ to repetition / short-range-Markov / long-range-positional signal; (3) a **per-instance sequentiality score s(u)** that gates a mixture-aware **router** between a sequential and an order-agnostic model; and (4) an **oracle headroom** bound on achievable sequential lift. A uniform full shuffle is the order-agnostic term's Monte-Carlo estimator, so the source paper's metric is SIEVE's special case (Boundary-Case Unification). Measured-not-Asserted: because the order-agnostic reference is approximate, Δ is a model-relative lower bound and an approximate headroom estimate, not an exact certificate.

## 4. The 5Q Research Proposal

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can we define and reliably estimate, for sequential-recommendation data, an instance-level order-dependence measure — the held-out predictive-information gap (in nats) between an order-aware model and a matched order-agnostic model, decomposed via nested shuffle/repeat nulls into repetition, short-range-Markov, and long-range-positional components — that subsumes the dataset-level shuffle test as its uniform-null special case, routes each user sequence to a sequential or order-agnostic recommender, and yields a conjectured, model-relative ceiling (estimated, not certified) on the accuracy any sequential model could gain over the best order-agnostic one?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

The problem SIEVE fixes is upstream of almost every SRS result: the entire empirical edifice of sequential recommendation rests on the unexamined premise that benchmark datasets actually *contain* the order-dependent signal the models exploit. Klenitskiy et al. (RecSys '24 LBR) showed via a shuffle test that several canonical datasets (Foursquare, Gowalla, RetailRocket, Steam, Yelp) carry little sequential structure — meaning years of "SOTA on dataset X" claims may have measured something other than sequence modeling. That makes the *measurement of order-dependence itself* a high-leverage object sitting upstream of model design, benchmark selection, leaderboard interpretation, and reproducibility. SIEVE turns a one-off diagnostic into a reusable, quantitative instrument, and shifts granularity from description to action via three moves. **Decomposition by signal type [designed]:** nested nulls separate genuine long-range positional order from repeat-consumption and short-range co-occurrence/popularity that survive partial shuffling — signals that call for different model classes, so conflating them is exactly how misleading "sequential" gains arise. **Instance-level scoring → mixture-aware routing [designed]:** a per-sequence score recognizes that a dataset is a *mixture* — some users order-driven, many order-agnostic — heterogeneity a scalar verdict hides, and routes each instance to the right model. **Oracle headroom bound [conjectured, model-relative ceiling — estimated, not certified]:** measured order-dependence is conjectured to upper-bound the achievable lift of any sequential model over the best order-agnostic one; because that reference model is approximated, this is a model-relative, decision-grade ceiling (Measured-not-Asserted), not an exact certificate. The broader implications are concrete. For **evaluation hygiene**, where the field has repeatedly found that benchmarks and protocols, not models, drive headline numbers (Ferrari Dacrema et al., RecSys 2019; Ludewig & Jannach 2018; Rendle et al. 2020), SIEVE gives curators a principled, decomposed admission criterion and a natural quantitative successor to the shuffle test. For **fair comparison**, a headroom bound contextualizes leaderboard deltas: a 0.3% NDCG gain where measured headroom is ~0.5% is near-ceiling, whereas the same gain where headroom is large is unimpressive. For **reproducibility**, per-instance scores and nested-null baselines are reproducible artifacts that travel with a dataset. Practically, SIEVE supports dataset selection by measured order-dependence rather than folklore, serves heavy sequence models only to users whose history warrants it (saving inference cost), and provides a pre-training go/no-go signal and compute-budget allocator before committing GPU-months to a large sequential recommender (the *size* of realized savings is **[conjectured]** pending experiments). In one line: SIEVE converts a binary "this dataset looks non-sequential" warning into a quantitative, decomposable, per-instance instrument that tells you whether to use a sequential model, where, and how much it could ever help.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

SIEVE sits on top of several genuinely difficult estimation and design problems, and naive shortcuts each fail for a concrete reason — which is exactly why a single dataset-level shuffle scalar is not enough.

*H1 — Predictive-information estimation on sparse, high-cardinality catalogs.* Δ is estimated as a difference of held-out per-token cross-entropies (nats) over catalogs of 10^4–10^6 items with short, sparse sequences. The estimate is high-variance and biased (cross-entropy on a 10^6-way softmax with few interactions per user has large sampling variance), and normalization-sensitive (Δ is a *difference* of two large NLLs, so small miscalibration in either model inflates or deflates it). Mitigation: report Δ relative to the order-agnostic NLL, use identical vocabularies, identical negative handling, and **full (unsampled) softmax** so the two terms cancel cleanly **[designed]**. A raw NLL gap without matched controls confounds model capacity with order-sensitivity.

*H2 — The matched-model confound (central methodological risk).* Δ measures *order* only if the order-aware and order-agnostic models differ in **exactly one thing**: sensitivity to permutation of the history. If they differ in parameter count, embedding dim, optimizer, regularization, or tuning budget, the gap is an architecture artifact. Mitigation: build the order-agnostic model as the **same backbone with positional information removed** (drop positional encodings / set-pool item embeddings via mean or sum), tie embedding tables, share hyperparameters, equalize tuning budget, and verify on a **placebo** (i.i.d. data) that Δ≈0 **[designed]**. This is the hardest control and the main threat to validity.

*H3 — Repeat-consumption and popularity masquerading as "sequence."* A model can predict the next item via repeat consumption or global popularity / short-range co-occurrence — none requiring true long-range order — and these survive partial shuffling. The **nested-null decomposition** (shuffle-all → window/block-shuffle of size w → de-duplicate) attributes Δ to {repeat-consumption, short-range Markov, long-range positional}; disentangling them is hard because they are correlated in real logs **[designed]**.

*H4 — Per-instance scores on short sequences are high-variance.* The per-user score s(u) is a predictive-information gap localized to one short sequence (nearly meaningless for length-3), requiring **empirical-Bayes shrinkage** toward the stratum mean with weights depending on sequence length and item frequencies, validated on synthetic data with known per-user order **[designed]**.

*H5 — The headroom bound is model-relative.* The "best order-agnostic model" is imperfect, so Δ is a **model-relative lower bound** on true order-dependence and an **approximate** headroom estimate, not an exact certificate. The Fano/data-processing argument that dataset-level Δ upper-bounds achievable sequential lift is **[conjectured]** and pre-registered as such, with its circularity (using models to bound what models can achieve) stated up front.

*H6 — Evaluation-protocol pitfalls.* Leave-one-out leaks future information and inflates sequential signal, so we use a **strict global-timeline temporal split**; sampled metrics can reorder methods (Krichene & Rendle 2020; Dallmann et al. 2021), so we use **full unsampled ranking metrics**; per-user scores come from **held-out folds only**, popularity de-biased.

*H7 — Routing must beat the right baselines.* Beating one pure model is insufficient; the gated hybrid must beat **both pure models AND a naive 50/50 ensemble** to prove that instance-level routing on s(u), not mere averaging, is what helps.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

SIEVE does not contradict prior work — it **subsumes and extends** it. Against the **source paper** (Klenitskiy, Volodkevich, Pembek & Vasilev, RecSys '24 LBR), which introduced the shuffle diagnostic and three estimators but yields a binary, dataset-level scalar with no signal-type separation and no per-instance decision rule, SIEVE strictly generalizes: a uniform full shuffle is a Monte-Carlo estimator of the order-agnostic term, so their metric is a **special case [provable-by-construction]** (order-0 data ⇒ gap = 0), to which we add decomposition, routing, and the headroom bound. Against **sequential architectures** (GRU4Rec, Hidasi et al., ICLR 2016; SASRec, Kang & McAuley, ICDM 2018; BERT4Rec, Sun et al., CIKM 2019; SR-GNN, Wu et al., AAAI 2019), which advance capacity but presume sequential data and report aggregate accuracy, SIEVE is **model-diagnostic, not another model** — it bounds what any architecture can gain and attributes signal to type. Against **evaluation/reproducibility critiques** (Ludewig & Jannach, UMUAI 2018; Ferrari Dacrema, Cremonesi & Jannach, RecSys 2019), which show simple baselines rival neural models and that protocol choices dominate, SIEVE supplies the missing *dataset-side* instrument and ties it to an achievable-performance ceiling. Against **protocol/metric pitfalls** (Krichene & Rendle, KDD 2020; Rendle, Krichene, Zhang & Anderson, RecSys 2020), which address *how we score models*, SIEVE estimates a data property *before* a model is scored, robust to those splitting/sampling choices. Against **SRS performance-revision work** (Klenitskiy & Vasilev, RecSys 2023), which shows fair training shrinks or reverses model gaps, SIEVE explains *why* such gaps can be illusory — when measured headroom is small, model differences cannot reflect genuine sequence modeling — and quantifies that headroom. Finally, the **information-theoretic / Markov-order tools** (entropy-rate and Markov-order estimation, predictive-information literature) existed in time-series/NLP/genomics but were **not ported to recommendation**, where huge high-cardinality catalogs, sparse long-tailed interactions, and short sequences make MI estimation statistically hard. The barriers that kept it unsolved: (1) the shuffle test was framed as a *binary* diagnostic, so the natural generalization — an information gap with *nested* nulls — was not pursued; (2) no instance-level / mixture framing, so heterogeneity and routing were never operationalized as a measurable score; (3) no bridge from "signal exists" to an achievable-performance ceiling, since diagnostics and leaderboards lived in separate worlds; and (4) MI / predictive-information estimation is genuinely hard on sparse, high-cardinality catalogs — a real deterrent that plausibly led the community to *avoid* rather than attempt it. SIEVE treats the estimate as a model-relative lower bound with nested-null calibration, converting an intractability into an honest, decision-grade approximation. Claims about the *magnitude* of compute savings and routing gains remain **[conjectured]** until measured.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

**Method (pipeline).** (1) **Matched-model predictive-information gap Δ** [provable-by-construction as an estimator]: Δ = I(next ; order-aware history) − I(next ; order-agnostic multiset), estimated as the difference of held-out per-token cross-entropies in nats; Δ>0 ⇒ order matters. (2) **Order-agnostic ablation** [designed]: same backbone (SASRec/GRU4Rec) with **positional encodings removed / set-pooled** (mean or sum), capacity- and tuning-matched, plus simple references (bag-of-items, ItemKNN, Popularity). (3) **Nested-null decomposition** [designed]: shuffle-all → **window/block-shuffle of size w** → **de-duplicate**, with successive differences attributing Δ to {repeat-consumption, short-range Markov, long-range positional}. (4) **Per-instance score s(u)** [designed]: localized held-out gap, **empirical-Bayes shrunk** for short sequences. (5) **Sequentiality-gated router** [designed]: route high-s(u) sequences to the SRS, low-s(u) to an order-agnostic model, threshold tuned on a held-out fold. (6) **Oracle headroom bound** [conjectured]: dataset-level Δ upper-bounds achievable lift of any sequential model over the best order-agnostic model, validated empirically via Spearman correlation. **Boundary-Case Unification** [provable-by-construction]: a uniform full-history shuffle is a Monte-Carlo estimator of the order-agnostic term, so the source paper's shuffle gap is a special case of Δ under the all-positions-uniform null; order-0 data ⇒ Δ=0; purely sequential data ⇒ all mass in the long-range component.

**Datasets.** Source-paper set (Foursquare, Gowalla, RetailRocket, Steam, Yelp); known-sequential controls (MovieLens-1M/20M, Amazon Beauty/Sports/Toys, Diginetica, YooChoose RecSys'15, Last.fm/30Music); ≥1 recent **public** large log (e.g., KuaiRec or Tenrec) for external validity — or, if a proprietary log is used, processed under a data-use agreement with user IDs hashed before SIEVE touches them; and a **synthetic generator** [designed] — a controllable mixture of **Markov-order-k** users + **i.i.d. popularity** users with **known ground-truth order and mixture fractions**. **Privacy:** all datasets are used in publicly released, anonymized forms with no re-identification attempted; Foursquare/Gowalla (location check-in logs) are handled at venue/category aggregation where individual-POI order is not required.

**Baselines/models.** Generalized target: the source paper's shuffle test. Order-aware: SASRec, GRU4Rec, BERT4Rec, SR-GNN. Order-agnostic: Popularity, ItemKNN, set-pooled no-position ablations. Router baseline: a **naive 50/50 ensemble** (must be beaten).

**Metrics.** Measure side: Δ in nats; component shares (repeat / short-range / long-range); per-user s(u) distribution. Downstream: NDCG@10, Recall@10, HR@10, MRR for router vs pure SRS vs pure baseline. Headroom validation: **Spearman rank-correlation** between dataset-level Δ and observed SRS-over-baseline NDCG lift. Efficiency: compute saved by skipping SRS where Δ≈0.

**Evaluation ladder.** (1) **Oracle (synthetic):** recover planted order and mixture fractions; verify s(u) calibration [provable-by-construction target]. (2) **Placebo (i.i.d. popularity):** Δ≈0, router never fires, headroom≈0 (falsifier for the H2 confound). (3) **Recovery (uniform shuffle of real data):** Δ collapses to the source paper's metric, reproducing the low-sequentiality verdict for Foursquare/Gowalla/RetailRocket/Steam/Yelp (Boundary-Case Unification check). (4) **Leakage controls:** strict global-timeline temporal split, per-user scores from held-out folds only, popularity de-biased, no future leakage. (5) **Router fairness/coverage:** break out NDCG@10 by **history-length decile** (and any available demographic proxy), and require the order-agnostic branch to be **at least as good as pure SRS for routed low-s(u) users**, so short-history users are not systematically underserved by routing. **Protocol:** full unsampled ranking metrics, fixed k-core preprocessing, multiple seeds, bootstrap confidence intervals, pre-registered analysis (including Δ's model-relativity and the conjectured headroom status).

**Expected outcomes (labeled).** Δ recovers and refines source-paper verdicts with component attribution [conjectured]; MovieLens/Diginetica/YooChoose show a large long-range component while Yelp/Gowalla show mostly repeat-consumption + short-range [conjectured]; the gated hybrid beats both pure models and the naive ensemble on heterogeneous datasets [conjectured]; Δ correlates strongly (Spearman) with realized SRS lift, supporting the headroom bound [conjectured]; compute is saved by skipping SRS where Δ≈0 [conjectured].

**Pre-registered honesty caveats (Measured-not-Asserted).** Δ is a model-relative lower bound on true order-dependence and an approximate headroom estimate — not an exact certificate. The headroom upper-bound (Fano/data-processing) is **[conjectured]** and validated only empirically via Spearman correlation. The decisive falsification risk: if the matched ablation is imperfect, Δ measures architecture, not order — hence the placebo and synthetic-recovery rungs are mandatory gates, run before any real-data claim.
